perturbation.original_text: As used herein, the following terms shall have the meanings ascribed to them below. "Action" has the meaning set forth in Section 8.1. "Affiliate" means, when used with respect to any specified Person, a Person that directly or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with, such specified Person. For the purposes of this definition, "control" (including the terms "controlled by" and "under common control with") with respect to the Source: CHINA REAL ESTATE INFORMATION CORP, F-1, 9/29/2009 relationship between or among two or more Persons, means the possession, directly or indirectly or as trustee, personal representative or executor, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the affairs or management of a Person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, as trustee, personal representative or executor, by contract, credit arrangement or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, the following terms shall have the meanings ascribed to them below. "Action" has the meaning set forth in Section 8.1. "Affiliate" means an associated entity. For the purposes of this definition, "control" means influence.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Affiliate" and "control" provided a detailed explanation of direct and indirect control through various means such as ownership, contract, etc. By omitting this detailed explanation and replacing it with vague terms like "associated entity" and "influence," the contract introduces ambiguity. This creates a contradiction because other sections rely on the original, precise definition to determine liabilities and rights. Specifically, Section 8.1 and 8.2 relies on the definition of affiliate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Indemnification by Licensor. Licensor shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Licensee and its Affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees, agents, shareholders, successors and assigns, (collectively, the "Licensee Parties") from and against any claim, suit, demand or action ("Action"), and any and all direct losses suffered or incurred by Licensee in connection with any third party claims arising out of or resulting from any breach by Licensor of any provision of this Agreement. Licensor's obligation to indemnify Licensee shall be conditioned on (a) Licensee's provision to Licensor of prompt notice of such an Action (except where any delay does not materially prejudice Licensor); (b) Licensee's reasonable cooperation with Licensor in the defense and settlement of such an Action at Licensor's cost; and (c) Licensor having exclusive control of the defense, settlement and/or compromise of such an Action (provided that Licensor may not settle any Action in a manner that adversely affects Licensee without Licensee's prior written consent, not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed).
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a detailed definition of 'Affiliate' and 'control', specifying various means of control such as ownership, contract, etc. The changed text replaces this precise definition with vague terms like 'associated entity' and 'influence'. This omission creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, which relies on the original, precise definition to determine the scope of Licensor's indemnification obligations to Licensee and its Affiliates. The changed definition introduces ambiguity, making it unclear which entities are covered under the indemnification clause, thus causing a potential contradiction in enforcement of liabilities and rights, and creating an inconsistency with the original contract. Since the indemnification by the Licensor depends on the definition of 'Affiliate' and the new definition is so vague, it creates a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Licensee shall monitor and censor all Content on the websites associated with the Licensed Domain Names, including without limitation Content posted by end users. Licensor shall also have the right to monitor and censor Content of the websites associated with Licensed Domain Names. Licensee shall remove any offending Content, including, but not limited to, any illegal materials, pornographic, obscene or sexually explicit materials, materials of a violent nature, or politically sensitive materials, from such websites as soon as possible after it becomes aware of such offending Content but in no event later than the timeframe prescribed by the Governmental Authority after receipt of oral or written notice from Licensor or such Governmental Authority.
perturbation.changed_text: Licensee shall monitor and censor all Content on the websites associated with the Licensed Domain Names, including without limitation Content posted by end users. Licensee shall remove any offending Content from such websites as soon as possible after it becomes aware of such offending Content.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that both Licensee and Licensor have the right to monitor and censor content, and it details the types of content considered 'offending.' By omitting the Licensor's right to monitor and censor and removing the detailed list of prohibited content, the modified clause creates a contradiction. Licensee's obligations remain, but the criteria for identifying 'offending Content' are undefined. This lack of definition creates uncertainty, especially considering Section 8.2, which deals with indemnification related to content.
perturbation.location: Section 3.3. Website Monitoring and Censoring.(a) Licensee Obligations
perturbation.contradicted_text: Licensee shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its Affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees, agents, shareholders, successors and assigns, (collectively, the "Licensor Parties") from and against any Action, and any and all direct losses suffered or incurred by Licensor in connection with any third party claims (a) arising out of or resulting from any breach by Licensee of any provision of this Agreement, (b) regarding the Content (other than Licensed Content) of the websites associated with Licensed Domain Names, or (c) regarding any Content that was subject to a request for removal by a Governmental Authority, even if Licensee removes such Content within the time period proscribed by the Governmental Authority, provided that, in all cases, Licensee shall not be liable for any direct losses suffered or incurred by Licensor as a result of Licensor's failure to provide Licensee with a reasonable period of time to remove Content in cases where (i) the basis or nature of the offense has not previously been identified by any Governmental Authority as offensive or inappropriate and (ii) Licensee has not also received notice from the Governmental Authority.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Licensor's right to monitor and censor content and eliminates the definition of 'offending content.' This directly contradicts the indemnification clause in the contradicted text, Section 8.2. Without a clear definition of 'offending content' or the Licensor's ability to monitor, the Licensee's obligation to defend and indemnify the Licensor against claims regarding Content becomes ambiguous and potentially unenforceable, as the Licensee may not know what constitutes 'offending content' that triggers the indemnification obligation. The Licensor's ability to monitor and identify such content is also removed. This is a strong contradiction as it fundamentally alters the basis for indemnification.

perturbation.original_text: The initial term of this Agreement (the "Initial Term") shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of ten (10) years thereafter. Beginning twelve (12) months prior to the expiration of the Initial Term, the Parties shall use 7 Source: CHINA REAL ESTATE INFORMATION CORP, F-1, 9/29/2009 good faith efforts to negotiate an extension of the term of this Agreement (the Initial Term together with any applicable extension, the "Term").
perturbation.changed_text: The initial term of this Agreement (the "Initial Term") shall commence on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of ten (10) years thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause regarding 'good faith efforts to negotiate an extension,' the contract omits a crucial aspect of the Term's potential duration. While the Initial Term is defined, the possibility of extending it is now absent. This creates an in-text contradiction because section 6.6 refers to termination and expiration of the agreement implying both exist, when there is no way to extend, only terminate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (a) Upon termination (but not expiration) of this Agreement for any reason, Licensee shall be entitled to use the Licensed Domain Names and Licensed Content for a limited period of time, not to exceed ninety (90) days, during which it shall diligently work to transition to another solution. Upon expiration of this Agreement or such 90-day period, (i) all rights granted to Licensee under this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Domain Names and Licensed Content shall immediately cease, and (ii) Licensee shall immediately discontinue all use of the Licensed Domain Names and Licensed Content.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for an extension of the agreement, which, along with termination, defined how the agreement could end. The changed text removes the possibility of extension, leaving only termination as a way for the agreement to end before the initial term. The contradicted text discusses both 'termination (but not expiration)' and 'expiration', which are no longer compatible because the agreement can now only terminate and not expire after the initial term. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists because a scenario described in the contradicted text ('expiration') is no longer possible based on the changed text.
